behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module handoff boundary (request builder -> API client payload). A unit test of only a helper can miss dropped/renamed fields during wiring/serialization. No real OS/network behavior is required, and correctness does not depend on model judgment, so System/Agentic are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the classifier module to a stub API client and assert outbound request payload always contains the full configured taxonomy labels (including edge cases like reordered input and duplicates removed per policy) before send.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is about response handoff (API response -> validator/normalizer -> public return). A pure unit test of normalization logic may miss integration bugs where raw provider value bypasses validation. No real infra or model-quality dependence is required, so System/Agentic are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Use a stub API returning out-of-taxonomy labels and verify module output is exactly "unknown" for caller-visible result across representative invalid values.

behaviors.description: For identical raw API responses, the module's normalization step always produces the same final label.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic local logic on identical inputs; failure boundary is one function/object (normalization). No module handoff, real infra, or model output quality is needed. Higher levels are rejected because behavior text explicitly fixes identical raw responses.
behaviors.test_description: Call normalization with repeated identical raw response fixtures (case, whitespace, punctuation variants already encoded in fixtures) and assert deterministic identical final label each run.

behaviors.description: On repeated live API calls for the same document and taxonomy, the returned label remains consistent unless the provider response itself changes.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on repeated live AI-provider decisions. A mock would hide model/provider variability and cannot validate consistency under real model outputs. Not Workflow because this is a single agentic decision repeated, not a multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run repeated real provider calls with the same document/taxonomy under controlled settings, record provider raw responses and final labels, and assert label stability when raw provider response is unchanged.

behaviors.description: Request construction enforces the configured token budget before dispatch (for example truncate/reject according to policy).
behaviors.justification: Failure typically appears across config -> tokenizer/counting -> request builder -> dispatch gate. A single-function test can miss miswired policy enforcement before send. Real provider/model output is not needed because enforcement is pre-dispatch; therefore System/Agentic are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: With module wired to a stub transport, feed documents around token thresholds and assert truncate/reject policy is applied before any outbound call is attempted.

behaviors.description: Provider-reported token usage for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: This requires a real AI provider call and real provider token accounting; stubs can falsely pass by returning expected usage. Per rule, execution through real AI/ML API remains Agentic even with deterministic assertions. Workflow is rejected because only one model call/decision path is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Execute real classification calls for representative documents and assert provider-reported usage fields are <= configured budget, failing if live usage exceeds budget despite local pre-checks.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Core risk is retry orchestration across client, backoff policy, and caller-facing error mapping. This can be caught with deterministic stub responses (e.g., 429 then success) and fake clock. Real model output is irrelevant, and real network/OS timing is not required for minimum failure detection; thus System/Agentic are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Use stub API sequence (rate-limit response with retry hint, then success), assert module waits per policy (with controllable clock), retries, and returns successful label instead of surfacing immediate error.
